content.text:
The Mean Value Theorem connects derivatives to overall behavior. It is 
one of the most powerful tools in analysis.

content.title: Interior Extremum Theorem
content.content:
If $f$ is differentiable at an interior point $c$ of its domain and $f$ 
has a local extremum at $c$, then $f'(c) = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $f$ has a local maximum at $c$. For small $h > 0$, 
$\frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \leq 0$. For small $h < 0$, 
$\frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \geq 0$. Both limits equal $f'(c)$, so $f'(c) = 0$.

content.title: Rolle's Theorem
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, differentiable on $(a, b)$, and 
$f(a) = f(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with $f'(c) = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $f$ is constant, any $c$ works. Otherwise, $f$ attains a maximum or 
minimum in $(a, b)$ (by the Extreme Value Theorem, and since 
$f(a) = f(b)$, the extreme value is not at both endpoints). Apply 
Theorem [thm:interior-extremum].

content.title: Mean Value Theorem
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and differentiable on $(a, b)$, then 
there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with
\[
f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Apply Rolle's Theorem to $g(x) = f(x) - \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}(x - a)$.

We have $g(a) = f(a)$ and $g(b) = f(b) - (f(b) - f(a)) = f(a) = g(a)$.

By Rolle, there exists $c$ with $g'(c) = f'(c) - \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a} = 0$.

content.title: Zero Derivative Implies Constant
content.content:
If $f$ is differentiable on $(a, b)$ and $f'(x) = 0$ for all $x \in (a, b)$, 
then $f$ is constant on $(a, b)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For any $x_1 < x_2$ in $(a, b)$, by MVT there exists $c$ with 
$f(x_2) - f(x_1) = f'(c)(x_2 - x_1) = 0$. So $f(x_1) = f(x_2)$.

content.title: Generalized Mean Value Theorem (Cauchy)
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are continuous on $[a, b]$ and differentiable on $(a, b)$, 
with $g'(x) \neq 0$ on $(a, b)$, then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with
\[
\frac{f'(c)}{g'(c)} = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{g(b) - g(a)}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: L'Hôpital's Rule
content.content:
Let $f$ and $g$ be differentiable on $(a, b)$ with $g'(x) \neq 0$. If 
$\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = \lim_{x \to a^+} g(x) = 0$ (or both $\pm\infty$), 
and $\lim_{x \to a^+} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)} = L$, then 
$\lim_{x \to a^+} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = L$.
content.label: Theorem
